allopack.title: Magnetism — Invisible Push and Pull
allopack.author: AlloFlow community catalog (authored by Claude, reviewed by A. Pomeranz)
allopack.standards: NGSS MS-PS2-3 (ask questions about data to determine the factors that affect the strength of electric and magnetic forces); NGSS MS-PS2-5 (conduct an investigation to provide evidence that fields exist between objects exerting forces on each other though the objects are not in contact); CCSS.ELA-LITERACY.RST.6-8.3 (follow a multistep procedure)

history.title: Feel the Invisible

data.body:
**Due:** (your teacher will tell you)

Hold two magnets close and you can feel something pushing back through empty air. Nothing is touching. That push is a **field**, and fields are one of the strangest true things in physics.

1. Read **The Push You Can Feel Through Nothing**.
2. Learn the terms in the **Glossary**, then win the **Matching** game.
3. Keep the **Magnet Rules** chart open while you experiment.
4. Sort the materials in **Will It Stick?** — three of these surprise people.
5. Take the quiz. Question 4 is the one almost everyone gets wrong.
6. If your class has the **Magnetism Lab** in the STEM Lab, run the Field Walk challenge and find the hidden magnet.

One rule for this lesson: every time you predict, say it OUT LOUD before you test. Being wrong out loud is how physics gets learned. 🧲

objectives.label: I found the hidden magnet in the Magnetism Lab Field Walk

history.meta: 6th-7th Grade • 4 goals • pairs with the STEM Magnetism Lab

history.title: The Push You Can Feel Through Nothing
history.data:
## The Push You Can Feel Through Nothing

Push two magnets together the wrong way and something invisible pushes back. Your hand feels it. Nothing is touching. This bothered scientists for centuries, and honestly it should bother you a little too.

Every magnet has two ends called **poles**: north and south. The rule is short and absolute — **opposite poles attract, like poles repel**. Bring two north poles together and they shove apart no matter how hard you press.

Here is a fact that surprises almost everyone: **you cannot make a magnet with only one pole.** Cut a bar magnet exactly in half hoping to separate north from south, and you do not get two halves. You get two complete smaller magnets, each with its own north and south. Cut those in half and it happens again. Physicists have searched hard for a single isolated pole — a magnetic monopole — and have never confirmed one.

The invisible push is described as a **magnetic field**: a region around the magnet where magnetic force acts. Sprinkle iron filings on paper over a magnet and they arrange into curved lines running pole to pole. You are not seeing the field itself — fields are not made of stuff — but you are seeing its shape.

Only a few materials are strongly magnetic. Iron, nickel, and cobalt are the common ones, which is why a refrigerator magnet grabs a steel door (steel is mostly iron) but slides right off an aluminum can, a copper pipe, or a gold ring. If a magnet does not stick to something metal, that is real information about which metal it is.

Now the connection that runs the modern world. In 1820, Hans Christian Ørsted noticed a compass needle twitch whenever he switched on a nearby electric current. Electricity and magnetism are not two separate phenomena — they are two faces of one thing. Coil a wire around an iron nail and run current through it and you have built an **electromagnet**, whose enormous advantage is that it can be switched OFF. That single property runs junkyard cranes, doorbells, speakers, hard drives, and every electric motor you have ever used.

And Earth itself is a giant magnet, generated by molten iron churning in its core. That is why a compass works. One last strange detail: the thing your compass calls "north" is physically a magnetic *south* pole — because opposite poles attract, and the needle's north end is being pulled toward it. Scientists have known about this naming joke for centuries and simply kept it.

data.def: An object that attracts iron and produces a magnetic field.
One of the two ends of a magnet, called north and south.
To pull toward — what opposite poles do.
To push away — what like poles do.
The region around a magnet where its force can act, even through empty space.
Able to be attracted by a magnet — true of iron, nickel, and cobalt.
A magnet made by running electric current through a coil of wire — it can be switched off.
The flow of electric charge through a wire.
A tool whose needle aligns with Earth's magnetic field to show direction.
A push or a pull acting on an object.
The center of Earth, where churning molten iron generates the planet's magnetism.

sections.label: POLES
bullets: Every magnet has north AND south
Cut one in half: you get TWO full magnets
sections.iconPrompt: a bar magnet split into two smaller labeled magnets

sections.label: FIELD
bullets: Acts through empty space — no touching
Iron filings reveal its curved shape
sections.iconPrompt: curved field lines around a bar magnet

sections.label: WHAT STICKS
bullets: Iron, nickel, cobalt (and steel, mostly iron)
NOT aluminum, copper, gold, or plastic
A magnet is a metal-identification tool
sections.iconPrompt: a magnet next to a steel nail and an aluminum can

sections.label: ELECTROMAGNET
bullets: Current in a coil makes a magnet
More coils or more current = stronger
The superpower: you can switch it OFF
sections.iconPrompt: a nail wrapped in wire connected to a battery

sections.label: EARTH
bullets: Molten iron core makes a planet-sized magnet
That is why a compass works
Geographic north is a magnetic SOUTH pole
sections.iconPrompt: a globe with field lines curving pole to pole

history.title: Will It Stick?

items.content: A nickel coin (mostly copper and nickel)
A stainless steel fork (varies by type)

history.meta: 2 buckets • 10 objects • the coin surprises everyone

history.title: Field Test

questions.question: You bring the north poles of two magnets together. What happens?
options: They push each other apart
They pull together strongly
Nothing — poles only matter for compasses
One pole flips to become a south pole

questions.question: You cut a bar magnet exactly in half. What do you have?
options: Two complete magnets, each with a north and a south pole
One north-only magnet and one south-only magnet
Two pieces of ordinary metal with no magnetism
One magnet twice as strong and one dead piece
questions.correctAnswer: Two complete magnets, each with a north and a south pole

questions.question: Why does a refrigerator magnet stick to a steel door but slide off an aluminum can?
options: Steel contains iron, which is magnetic; aluminum is not
The can is too smooth for the magnet to grip
Aluminum blocks magnetic fields entirely
The door is colder than the can
questions.correctAnswer: Steel contains iron, which is magnetic; aluminum is not

questions.question: The needle end of a compass that points toward geographic north is attracted to what?
options: A magnetic SOUTH pole — because opposite poles attract
A magnetic north pole, matching the name
The North Star's gravity
Nothing — compasses use gravity, not magnetism
questions.correctAnswer: A magnetic SOUTH pole — because opposite poles attract

questions.question: What is the single biggest practical advantage of an electromagnet over a permanent magnet?
options: It can be switched off and on
It never loses strength over time
It works without any metal nearby
It attracts every material equally
questions.correctAnswer: It can be switched off and on

questions.question: A junkyard crane picks up a car, moves it, then drops it. Explain why an electromagnet is required for this job and a permanent magnet would not work.
questions.expectedAnswer: The crane has to RELEASE the car. An electromagnet is only magnetic while current flows, so cutting the current drops the load. A permanent magnet would hold the car and could not let go on command.

questions.question: The reading says a magnetic field acts 'through empty space with no touching.' Describe one experiment you could do to show this is true.
questions.expectedAnswer: Place a paperclip on a table and slowly bring a magnet near without touching it — the clip jumps. Or hold a magnet under a sheet of paper or a table and move a clip on top. The magnet moves the clip across a gap, so the force crosses empty space.

reflections.text: What surprised you most: the cut-magnet result, or that north is really south? Explain.
List three things in your home that would stop working without electromagnets.

history.meta: 5 MCQ + 2 short answer • Q4 is the naming trap

data.question: Can magnets really erase a phone or a credit card? 💳
data.answer: Old magnetic-stripe cards, yes — a strong magnet can scramble the stripe. Modern phones and chip cards are far more resistant, though very strong magnets can still confuse a phone's compass sensor temporarily.

data.question: Why do magnets get weaker over time?
data.answer: Heat, hard impacts, and stray opposing fields gradually knock the tiny aligned regions inside out of formation. Drop a magnet enough times and you genuinely weaken it.

data.question: Has Earth's magnetic field ever flipped? 🌍
data.answer: Many times — the rock record shows north and south have swapped repeatedly over millions of years. The last full reversal was roughly 780,000 years ago. It takes thousands of years to complete, not a day.

data.question: Is there such a thing as a one-pole magnet?
data.answer: None has ever been confirmed. Some theories predict magnetic monopoles should exist, and physicists have run serious searches for them, but so far every magnet ever cut has produced two poles.

data.question: Are humans magnetic?
data.answer: Barely — living tissue is very weakly repelled by strong fields. Scientists have levitated a live frog in an extremely powerful magnet, which is real and as strange as it sounds. The frog was unharmed.

data.question: Why do speakers need magnets? 🔊
data.answer: A coil carrying a changing current sits in a magnet's field. The changing current makes the coil push and pull against that field, vibrating a cone and creating sound. Your music is literally magnetism moving air.
